response:
The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

model_message.content: <|im_end|>

response: Converts mask images into ROI by applying a connected components algorithm and generates measurements. Please note that this algorithm will always trace the external object boundaries and convert them into polygonal ROIs. This means that holes will be closed. This node requires a thresholded image as input and will extract measurements from the thresholded image. If you already have ROI available and want measurements, use 'Extract ROI statistics'.If higher-dimensional data is provided, the results are generated for each 2D slice.

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.

  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

response:
The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. First the radial distribution of the object is computed (see plugin 3D Radial
Distribution), as concentric circles centred on the seed define growing
regions of interest in which mean intensity values are measured. A
gaussian fit of the radial distribution is computed in a given radius around the seed (Radius Max, in pixels)). The standard deviation of the fitted
gaussian curve is used to define the threshold. The user enters a factor
which is applied to the standard deviation to define the value of the
threshold (sd value). As a rule of the thumb, a factor 1.17 will bring the
threshold to the full width at half maximum while factors 2 and 3 will fill
about 90% and 99% of the curve surface, respectively.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: segment cell with overlapping regions and touching cells

response:
Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: Automatically detect regions of interest in microscopy images

response:
Applies a pre-trained CLIJx-Weka model to an image and a corresponding label map to classify labeled objects.

Given radii allow to configure if values of proximal neighbors, other labels with centroids closer
than given radius, should be taken into account, e.g. for determining the regional maximum.

Make sure that the handed over feature list and radii are the same used while training the model.

Parameters: Image input, Image label_map, ByRef Image destination, String features, String modelfilename, Number radius_of_maximum, Number radius_of_minimum, Number radius_of_mean, Number radius_of_standard_deviation
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

b17a9e02-8b9f-4cbc-b5b6-5bdb417ab945.prompt: I want to quantify the co-localization of two different markers within segmented objects. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that first segments objects, then measures the intensity of both markers within each object, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson's or Manders') for each object, outputting these coefficients alongside other object measurements?

model_message.thinking:
Okay, let's tackle this user's request. They want a JIPipe pipeline that quantifies co-localization between two markers in segmented objects. The steps are: segment objects, measure intensity of both markers in each object, calculate a co-localization coefficient, and output all these metrics.

First, I need to figure out the necessary nodes. The user mentioned segmentation, so I should start with a segmentation node. The get_node_name tool can help here. The prompt should be something like "segment objects" or "segment images into objects". Let's use "segment objects" as the prompt.

Once the segmentation node is found, the next step is to measure the intensity of both markers. The user wants both markers' intensities measured within each object. So another node for intensity measurement would be needed. The prompt for that could be "measure intensity of markers within segmented objects".

After that, calculating the co-localization coefficient. The Manders' coefficient or Pearson's might be the right ones. The tool for that would be something like "calculate co-localization coefficient". The input prompt should specify the markers and the method.

Finally, outputting all measurements. The user wants these coefficients alongside other object measurements. So maybe a node for outputting results. The prompt here could be "output measurements".

I need to make sure each node is correctly identified and the connections are made between them. Let's check the tools again. The get_node_name function gives the node id and description. Once those are confirmed, the check_connection function can verify if the edges are present.

Putting it all together, the pipeline should start with segmentation, then intensity measurement for both markers, co-localization coefficient calculation, and finally outputting the results. Each step needs to be connected via edges. Let me structure the nodes and edges accordingly.

response:
The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: segment individual crystals in each frame

response:
The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

response: This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. response:
Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: apply denoising to enhance weak and noisy signals

response:
Applies the bilateral filter to an image.

The function applies bilateral filtering to the input image, as described in http://www.dai.ed.ac.uk/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/MANDUCHI1/Bilateral_Filtering.html bilateralFilter can reduce unwanted noise very well while keeping edges fairly sharp. However, it is very slow compared to most filters.

Sigma values: For simplicity, you can set the 2 sigma values to be the same. If they are small (< 10), the filter will not have much effect, whereas if they are large (> 150), they will have a very strong effect, making the image look "cartoonish".

Filter size: Large filters (d > 5) are very slow, so it is recommended to use d=5 for real-time applications, and perhaps d=9 for offline applications that need heavy noise filtering.

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number radiusX, Number radiusY, Number radiusZ, Number sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

1. **Denoising**: Apply the `clij:plugin:net.haesleinhuepf.clijx.plugins.NonLocalMeans` node to enhance weak and noisy signals.
2. **Segmentation**: Use the `segment_protein_and_organelle` node to accurately identify the fluorescent protein and organelle in each frame.
3. **Coefficient Calculation**: Calculate co-localization using the `co_localization_calculator` node with Pearson's or Manders' coefficient.
4. **Output**: Generate a time-series of co-localization values for each cell at each time point.

The pipeline ensures accurate signal enhancement, segmentation, and coefficient calculation for the requested analysis.<|im_end|>

response:
Author: Robert Haase, based on work by Loic A. Royer

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number radiusX, Number radiusY, Number radiusZ, Number sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: segmenting fluorescent protein and organelle in each frame

response:
Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filters to enhance signal

1. **Denoising**:

2. **Segmentation**:  
   - **Node**: `segmentation_node`  
   - **Function**: Segments fluorescent protein and organelle in each frame.  

3. **Co-Localization Coefficient**:  
   - **Node**: `co_localization_coefficient_node`  
   - **Function**: Calculates co-localization coefficients (e.g., Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point.

- `ij1-filter-kuwahara2d` → `segmentation_node` (signal → segmentation)

This pipeline ensures signal enhancement, accurate segmentation, and efficient co-localization analysis.<|im_end|>

arguments.prompt: segmentation of fluorescent protein and organelle

response: This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

response:
Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

d12c4663-7fd5-4893-923e-57ad1bce71c0.final_output:
<|endoftext|>In the JIPipe pipeline, the steps are as follows:

1. **Denoising**: The `clij:plugin:net.haesleinhuepf.clijx.plugins.NonLocalMeans` node applies non-local means filtering to the images, reducing noise and enhancing features.

2. **Segmentation**: The `segment` node (with the same tool response) is used to accurately identify the fluorescent protein and organelle in each frame.

3. **Co-localization Calculation**: The `co_localize` node calculates the co-localization coefficient (e.g., Pearson or Manders' coefficient) for each cell at each time point.

The pipeline outputs a time-series of co-localization values.<|im_end|>
